title: Service Lifetimes
use_for: aligning services with host and module lifecycle boundaries
choosing singleton, scoped, or transient ownership intentionally
keeping lifetime decisions explicit at composition time
do_not_use_for: general contract design without lifecycle concerns
feature-local object creation with trivial ownership
rules: assign service lifetimes explicitly and justify them
align lifetime policy with the host lifecycle and composition seam
avoid longer-lived services depending on shorter-lived state implicitly
treat scoped lifetime as a real ownership boundary, not a naming convention
keep lifetime mismatches visible during composition review
procedure: Classify each service by the lifecycle boundary it should live within.
Define singleton, scoped, or transient policy intentionally.
Check dependencies for lifetime inversion or leakage.
Define disposal and reset expectations per lifetime class.
Verify the policy remains valid across host, module, and runtime phases.

primary_when: the request is specifically about singleton versus scoped versus transient policy
the request is about lifetime mismatches, scope boundaries, or ownership across runtime phases
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about contract shape, feature-local construction, or extension hook design with no lifetime problem

solves: how long services should live and who owns that duration
how to prevent long-lived services from leaking shorter-lived state
how to align composition policy with real runtime boundaries
does_not_solve: whether a contract should exist in the first place

core_principles: A lifetime is an ownership boundary, not just a container label.
Long-lived services must not quietly capture shorter-lived state.
Lifetime policy should reflect real host and module phases, not convenience defaults.
constraints: Lifetime rules must remain inspectable at the composition boundary.
Disposal or reset expectations should exist for every non-transient lifetime.
Scoped policies should map to real runtime or request boundaries.
decision_rules: Use service-lifetimes as the owner when the core question is how service duration aligns with host, module, or request boundaries.
If the mismatch arises at a larger host-module seam, attach composition-boundaries before patching registrations.
If lifetime policy depends on runtime phases, attach runtime-lifecycle to anchor scope boundaries explicitly.
If a service needs data from a shorter-lived scope, prefer a boundary redesign over quiet capture.

preferred_patterns: lifetime classes mapped to real runtime boundaries
mismatch review during registration and composition
clear disposal or reset policy per non-transient service
avoid_patterns: scopes that exist only in container configuration with no runtime meaning
transient churn used as a substitute for clear ownership
lifetime defaults applied without architectural justification

recipes.name: Audit service lifetime mismatches
recipes.when: Use when a composed runtime has unclear ownership or services outlive the state they depend on.
steps: List services by intended lifetime boundary.
Find dependencies that point from longer-lived services to shorter-lived ones.
Redesign the seam or adjust lifetime policy explicitly.
Define disposal and reset behavior for remaining long-lived services.

pitfalls: Treating singleton, scoped, and transient as container defaults rather than architecture choices.
Allowing long-lived services to retain short-lived state implicitly.
Creating scopes that have no real runtime meaning.
review_checklist: Are lifetimes mapped to real runtime boundaries?
Do longer-lived services avoid shorter-lived state capture?
Are scoped boundaries explicit and meaningful?
Is disposal or reset policy defined where needed?

references.title: Local Reference: Service Lifetimes
references.interpretation: Use this first for lifetime classes, mismatch review, and ownership boundaries across composed services.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for service lifetime policy.

references.title: Local Reference: Framework DI
references.interpretation: Use this to keep lifetime policy tied to explicit registration and composition-root ownership.
references.why: Use to align lifetime choices with DI and registration flow.
